screen.name: screen1
screen.title: Bullet from McCoy’s gun – Class characteristics
screen.desc: What is the caliber of this bullet? Use the scale to measure the caliber.
screen.response: When determining the caliber, you need to measure the width of the bullet, not the length. Try again.
The scale is in millimeters, not centimeters. Try again.

examples.title: Bullet from McCoy’s gun – Class characteristics
examples.desc:
Most modern guns have spiral grooves inside their barrels, which are called RIFLING.
When a bullet is fired, rifling makes the bullet spin in the air. Rifling leaves impressions on the surface of the bullet, which can either be conventional or polygonal.
examples.popup: Conventional rifling leaves deep, easy to see grooves on the surface of the bullet.
Polygonal rifling leaves impressions that are less easy to see, with less distinct grooves.

screen.name: screen2
screen.title: Bullet from McCoy’s gun – Class characteristics
screen.desc: What type of rifling impressions does this bullet have?
screen.response: Yes, the grooves are very distinct, so we’re looking at conventional rifling.
If this rifling were polygonal, you couldn’t see the grooves that well. Try again.
No, rifling is either one or the other. Try again.

screen.name: screen3
screen.title: Bullet from McCoy's gun - Class characteristics
screen.desc: How many rifling impressions does this bullet have?
screen.caption: Previous Groove
Next Groove
screen.response:
Not quite.
Try rotating the bullet if you’re not sure and try again.

Not quite.
Try rotating the bullet if you’re not sure and try again.

screen.name: screen4
screen.title: CLASS CHARACTERISTICS - SUMMARY
screen.response: Looks like a match to me – all three class characteristics are the same.

screen.name: screen5
screen.title: Bullet from McCoy’s gun - Individual characteristics
screen.desc:
When a bullet is shot, the barrel of the gun leaves tiny, microscopic scratches on its surface.
These scratches are called STRIATIONS.

screen.name: screen6
screen.text:
No two firearms will produce exactly the same striations. If the striations match, we’ll have our murder weapon.
Comparing striations is not easy and takes years of training. Give it your best shot.
screen.title: COMPARISON MICROSCOPE
screen.instruct: Click here when the suspect bullet's groove matches the crime scene bullet's groove
screen.help: Slide the slider to focus the bullet you have loaded
Move the loaded bullet to align the groove mark with the autopsy bullet
Click here when you think the chosen suspect's bullet groove matches the autopsy bullet's groove
screen.response: The striations don’t look exactly the same. Try again.
There are small differences between the two bullets. Try again.

No, these striations don’t look the same.
Would you like me to take it from here?
screen.noMatch:
That’s right, the striations don’t match.
Why don't you try checking another groove?

I don’t see a match either.
Try another groove.

Correct, the striations are different.
Would you like me to take it from here?
screen.finish:
It seems that none of the grooves match.
Let me finish up.
